_provenance: Claude-authored original passages (DeepSeek not used). Gaokao-level reading, 4 MCQs each (detail/inference/attitude/main idea). Passages lengthened to 200-280 words for gaokao-reading-mcq template; only passage sentences were added, the 4 questions/options/answers are unchanged.

sets.passage: For decades, the night train seemed like a relic of the past. Cheap flights had lured travellers away, and one by one, Europe's sleeper services were shut down. Recently, however, the night train has begun an unexpected comeback. Worried about the climate impact of flying, a growing number of travellers are choosing to fall asleep in one city and wake up in another. New routes now connect Vienna with Paris, and Stockholm with Hamburg, often booked out weeks in advance. For many, the appeal lies in the slow rhythm of the journey itself. Instead of rushing through a crowded airport, passengers can share a meal in the dining car, watch the landscape darken outside the window, and slowly drift off to the gentle motion of the train. Operators admit the business is not easy. Sleeper carriages are expensive to maintain, and a single delayed train can upset an entire timetable. Yet demand keeps rising, especially among younger passengers who see an overnight journey not as wasted time but as part of the adventure. 'You go to bed in one country and open the curtains on another,' one regular traveller said. 'No airport can give you that.' Governments, too, are taking notice, with several now offering financial support to keep the routes running. Whether this revival can last is still uncertain, but for now the night train has reclaimed a place in the imagination of a generation that once believed it belonged only to old films and faded postcards.

questions.prompt: Why did many of Europe's night trains close in the past?
What mainly explains the night train's recent comeback?
options: Concern about the climate impact of flying.
A sharp fall in ticket prices.
Sleeper carriages are costly and delays disrupt timetables.

questions.prompt: What is the traveller's attitude toward overnight journeys?

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B：廉价航班抢走客流)、主旨/原因(A：担心飞行的气候影响)、细节(B：卧铺车厢贵、晚点扰乱时刻表)、态度(A：欣赏其独特体验)。

sets.passage: When most people picture a scientist, they imagine someone who rarely makes mistakes. Dr. Elena Fischer, a marine biologist, wants to change that picture. For the past five years, she has kept what she calls a 'failure log' — a public notebook listing every experiment that went wrong. Each entry is short but honest: what she expected, what actually happened, and what she finally learned from the result. At first, her colleagues were puzzled. Why advertise your worst moments? But Fischer believes that hiding failure does real harm. Young researchers, she argues, often give up because they think everyone else succeeds easily. By showing how many attempts lie behind a single discovery, she hopes to make science feel less frightening. Her log has now been read by thousands of students around the world. Many write to thank her, saying it gave them the courage to try again after their own experiments failed. 'A failed experiment is not a wasted one,' Fischer often tells them. 'It is simply one more way of learning how the world really works.' For her, the point is not to celebrate failure for its own sake, but to strip away the shame that so often surrounds it. If young scientists can learn to see a setback as information rather than humiliation, she believes, then curiosity, rather than fear, will become the force that drives their work forward.

options: A list of her most successful experiments.
A public notebook of experiments that went wrong.
A diary of her daily routine.
A guide to marine biology for beginners.

questions.prompt: Why were her colleagues puzzled at first?
options: They thought the log was too long.
They wondered why she would advertise her failures.
They believed the experiments were dangerous.
They could not understand her notes.

questions.prompt: According to Fischer, why do young researchers often give up?
options: They think everyone else succeeds easily.

questions.prompt: What does Fischer mean by 'A failed experiment is not a wasted one'?
options: Failure is a normal part of learning.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B：记录失败实验的公开本)、细节(B：不解她为何公开失败)、细节(A：以为别人都轻易成功)、推断(B：失败是学习的一部分)。
